cards.title: Your AI Brain Just Went Offline? The Wild West of LLM Quotas is Burning Builders
cards.thesis: People building cool stuff with AI (like using tools that write code or generate text) are getting seriously burned by the companies providing these AI brains. Their access to powerful AI models can suddenly disappear because quotas (limits on how much AI you can use) are changed without warning, or their prepaid money for using the AI vanishes. This makes it super hard to build reliable products because you can't trust the underlying AI services to stay available or predictable.

evidence.text: Suddenly our Opus 4.7 access was removed from Bedrock – the quota (limit on usage) was set to 0. This isn’t the first time I’ve faced this issue. Looks like AWS can revoke access to frontier models anytime without any warnings.

My Claude Pro subscription lapsed and the prepaid Extra Usage balance was gone with it! Anthropic's docs tie Extra Usage to an active Pro plan but don't spell out what happens to unused credits on cancellation or expiry. No warning or payout option I would have noticed.

If you put your Anthropic API key (the secret code that lets your app talk to the AI) directly in your cloud environment, Claude Code will fail to do anything. This caused a lot of 'Extra usage' charges for me.

cards.opportunity: Everyone's complaining about LLM providers like Anthropic silently changing quotas or eating up prepaid credits – but nobody's built a simple dashboard that lets you track real-time usage, set soft caps across different LLM APIs (the way your app talks to the AI), and get clear alerts *before* your access gets cut or your credits disappear. You could build a basic version this weekend by hooking into a few LLM APIs (like OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini) and a simple database to store usage logs, giving builders peace of mind and preventing sudden outages.

cards.title: DeFi's Next Big Bet: Making Stablecoins Trustworthy
cards.thesis: Smart contracts (self-executing digital agreements on a blockchain) are the foundational code for decentralized finance. We're seeing active development in the core infrastructure for stablecoins (digital currencies designed to hold a stable value) and stable pools, indicating a continuous need for robust and transparent financial plumbing in the crypto world.

evidence.text: TQ-trade's 'rari-stable-pool-contracts' — the underlying code for a key stable asset pool — is getting attention with 141 engagements on GitHub.
Similarly, their 'defidollar-core' smart contracts for the $DUSD stablecoin show strong interest with 139 engagements on GitHub, pointing to ongoing development in core stablecoin tech.

cards.opportunity: New stablecoin and stable pool projects are constantly launching, but they all struggle with the same thing: earning user trust. The first person to ship a plug-and-play widget or API (a way for different software to talk to each other) that automatically pulls and displays key health metrics like collateralization ratio and total value locked for any given stablecoin project will own the 'trust layer' for emerging DeFi. You could get an MVP live this weekend by scraping public blockchain data.
